utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Hey Carmen! Good morning! How are you today?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Hi Miguel! I am very good, thank you! And you?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: I'm great, thanks for asking! So, how was your weekend? Did you do anything fun?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh yes! My weekend was very nice. I go to... how to say... Camden Market?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Oh nice, Camden Market! That's a great place. So you went to Camden Market - remember, for the past, we say 'I went', not 'I go'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Ah yes, I went to Camden Market. Is very nice there!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It is very nice there, yes! Remember we need 'it' at the beginning - 'It is very nice'. What did you buy?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I buy... I bought... a scarf? Yes, a scarf for my mother. Is very cold today in London!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great self-correction with 'bought'! And yes, it IS very cold. We always need that 'it' - 'It is very cold'. English always needs a subject.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: It is very cold. Okay, I understand. In Spain is not so cold, you know?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: In Spain it is not so cold, right! Are you getting used to the London weather?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Hmm, getting used? What is this?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Getting used to - it means acostumbrarse. Like, is the weather okay for you now, or is it still strange?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Ah! Acostumbrarse! No, I am not... acostumbrada... I am not used to! I miss the sun of Spain.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! I totally understand. Okay, so today we have a really fun topic. We're going to talk about family and home. La familia y el hogar.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh, I love this topic! I love my family very much. I miss them.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Aw, I'm sure! Well, this is perfect then because you can tell me all about them. But first, let me ask - do you know some family words in English already?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! I know mother, father, sister, brother... and... mmm... baby?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great start! Baby is a good word too. Okay, so today we'll learn all the family members and also how to describe them. Sound good?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes, sounds very good!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Awesome. And then after family, we'll talk about your home - the rooms in a house. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Ready! Estoy lista!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! In English, please! Okay, let's start. So how was life in London this week? Are you enjoying the university?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes, I like the university. Actually I live in London for three months now.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Interesting! So be careful with 'actually' - in English it doesn't mean 'actualmente'. 'Actually' means 'en realidad'. If you want to say 'actualmente', you say 'currently'. Currently I live in London.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh! This is very confusing. Currently. Currently I live in London. Okay.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes, it's a classic trap between Spanish and English. Don't worry, everyone makes that mistake. So you've been in London for three months - that's exciting!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! I go in the university every day and I have many friends from different countries.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: That sounds wonderful! Just one small thing - we say 'I go TO the university', not 'in'. 'To' for the direction, 'in' for when you're already inside.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I go to the university. Hmm, in Spanish we say 'en' for everything!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! That's why it's tricky. Okay, are you ready to dive into family vocabulary?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! I want to talk about my family.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect! Let's begin with the basics. You already said 'mother' and 'father'. In everyday English, most people say 'mum' or 'mom' and 'dad'. Have you heard those?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! Mom and dad. Like in the movies. My mom is called... her name is Ana.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Her name is Ana, lovely! Now, do you have brothers or sisters?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! I have the sister and the brother.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Okay, close! In English, when we talk about family for the first time, we say 'a sister and a brother', not 'the'. We use 'a' because it's new information. 'I have a sister and a brother.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I have a sister and a brother. Why not 'the'? In Spanish we say 'la hermana'...

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good question! In English, 'the' is for when you and the listener both know which one. Since I don't know your sister yet, you say 'a sister'. Once we talk about her, THEN we can say 'the sister' or better, her name.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Okay, I understand. I have a sister. She is called Lucia.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great! Now, let's also learn: grandmother and grandfather. Do you know those?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Grandmother is... abuela? Grand-mother?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! Grandmother - abuela. And grandfather - abuelo. We can also say 'grandma' and 'grandpa' informally.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Grandma and grandpa! Oh, that is nice. My grandma... she is very old but very funny.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: That's a great sentence! Okay, now some more. Aunt - do you know this one?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Aunt is... tia? Like... A-U-N-T?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes! Aunt. And uncle - tio. And their children are your cousins. 'Cousin' - primo or prima.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Cousin! I have many cousins. In Spain, the family is very big. I have... mmm... twelve cousins!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Twelve cousins! Wow, that IS a big family. Okay, let me check - can you tell me all the family words we know now? Let's list them.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Okay. Mother, father, sister, brother, grandmother, grandfather, aunt, uncle, cousin. Yes?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Excellent! All correct. Now let me teach you a few more. Husband and wife - esposo y esposa.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Husband and wife. My mother and father, they are husband and wife.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! And do you know 'nephew' and 'niece'? Sobrino y sobrina.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Nephew and niece! I am not... I don't have nephew or niece because my sister and brother are young.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect sentence! 'I don't have a nephew or niece because my sister and brother are young.' Really well done.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you! I try!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: You're doing great. Now, let's practice with a question. The question is: 'Do you have any brothers or sisters?' This is a very common question in English. Can you answer?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Do I have... Yes, I have a sister and a brother. My sister is Lucia and my brother is Pablo.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great! Now I'll ask another way: 'How many people are in your family?'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In my family there is... there are... five persons. My mother, my father, my sister, my brother, and me.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Really good! And I love that you corrected yourself from 'there is' to 'there are' - because five is plural. One small thing: we say 'five people', not 'five persons'. 'People' is the normal word.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Five people. Okay. But in my big family, with grandparents and aunts and uncles and cousins, we are... maybe thirty people!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Thirty people! That's amazing. Do you all get together for holidays?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh yes! In Christmas and in... es que... how to say 'Semana Santa'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Semana Santa is 'Easter' or 'Holy Week' in English. 'At Christmas and at Easter' - and notice we say 'at Christmas', not 'in Christmas'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: At Christmas and at Easter. So many prepositions! This is very difficult.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: I know, prepositions are the hardest part! But you're doing well. Okay, let me now ask you about specific family members. Tell me about your sister Lucia.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My sister Lucia, she have sixteen years.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Okay, careful! This is a big difference between Spanish and English. In Spanish you say 'tiene dieciseis anos' - she HAS sixteen years. But in English, we say 'she IS sixteen years old'. We use 'be', not 'have' for age.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She is sixteen years old! That is so strange. Why you don't have years in English?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Good question. It's just a different way of thinking. English says 'you ARE an age', Spanish says 'you HAVE years'. And notice your question - 'Why DON'T you' not 'Why you don't'. The auxiliary verb comes before the subject in questions.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Why don't you... okay. And my brother Pablo, he is twelve years old.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect! You got it that time. And what about your parents? How old are they?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My mother she is forty-five years old. And my father, he have... he IS fifty years old.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Nice self-correction! 'He is fifty years old.' Your dad is fifty and your mom is forty-five. Great. Now what do your parents do? What are their jobs?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My father work in a hospital. He is... enfermero... how to say?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Enfermero is 'nurse' in English. 'My father is a nurse.' And remember, with he/she, we add -s to the verb: 'My father workS in a hospital.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My father works in a hospital. He is a nurse. And my mother, she is a teacher. She teach... she teaches mathematics.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Excellent! 'She teaches mathematics.' You remembered the -s that time! Carmen, you're a fast learner.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you! Is because I practice a lot. I watch Netflix in English every day!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: IT is because - remember our friend 'it'! 'It is because I practice a lot.' And watching Netflix is a great strategy!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: It is because I practice. Yes, I know, I always forget the 'it'.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It's totally normal for Spanish speakers. In Spanish you can just say 'es porque' without a subject, but English always needs one. You'll get it with practice. Now, let's learn a few more useful questions for talking about family. Repeat after me: 'Where does your father work?'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Where does your father work?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great! Now answer it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My father works in a hospital in Valencia.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect! And another question: 'What does your mother do?'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My mother is a teacher. She teaches mathematics in a... escuela... a school.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Well done! 'School' is the word. Okay, now one more family member. Do you have any pets? Mascotas?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh yes! We have a dog! His name is Chico. He is very small and very... loco... how to say 'loco'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! 'Loco' is 'crazy' in English. 'He is very small and very crazy.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He is very small and very crazy! Yes! He run in the house all day.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: He RUNS in the house - remember the -s for he/she/it. 'He runs in the house all day.' That sounds like a fun dog!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He runs in the house all day. Yes, I miss Chico very much.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: I'm sure! Okay, do you also have cats?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: No, we don't have cats. My mother don't like cats.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Almost! 'My mother DOESN'T like cats.' With he/she, the negative is 'doesn't', not 'don't'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My mother doesn't like cats. Okay. But my aunt, she have two cats. They are very beautiful.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: She HAS two cats. 'Have' becomes 'has' with he/she/it. 'My aunt has two cats.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She has two cats. Has, has, has. I need to remember this!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: You will! It just takes practice. Okay, great job with family vocabulary so far! Now let's move to something important - possessive adjectives. Do you know what those are?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Possessive... adjetivos posesivos? My, your...?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! My, your, his, her, its, our, their. These are super important for talking about family. Let's go through them. 'My' - I think you know this one well.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! My mother, my father, my sister, my brother. My family!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect. Now 'your' - this is for the person you're talking to. 'Your name is Carmen.' Easy, right?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Your name is Miguel! Yes, I know 'your'.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great! Now the tricky ones: 'his' and 'her'. In Spanish, 'su' is the same for he and she. But in English, we have two different words. 'His' for a man, 'her' for a woman.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh, this is difficult. So... his is for a man. Her is for a woman.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! So if we talk about your father, we say 'HIS name is...' And your mother? 'HER name is...' Try it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: His name is... my father, his name is Carlos. And my mother, her name is Ana.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Beautiful! Perfect use of his and her. Now let me ask you - what is your brother's name?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Her name is... no! His name is Pablo. His because Pablo is a boy.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Excellent catch! You corrected yourself. Pablo is a boy, so 'his name is Pablo.' And your sister?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Her name is Lucia. She is sixteen years old.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect! You're getting this. Now let's make some longer sentences. Can you say: 'My mother, her name is Ana, and she is forty-five years old'?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My mother, her name is Ana, and she is forty-five years old. She is a teacher.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Wow, you added extra information! Great job. Now try one about your father.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My father, his name is Carlos, and he is fifty years old. He is a nurse and he works in a hospital.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Carmen, that's a fantastic sentence! Really well structured. I'm impressed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you! I am very... Ay, como se dice 'orgullosa'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Orgullosa is 'proud'! 'I am very proud.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I am very proud! Yes!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: You should be! Now, let's practice possessives with a little game. I'll ask a question and you answer with the correct possessive. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Ready!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Where does your grandmother live?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My grandmother, she live in a small village near Valencia. Her village is very beautiful.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good use of 'her'! But remember: she LIVES - with an -s. 'She lives in a small village.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She lives in a small village. The -s always! I forget always.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: 'I always forget' - in English the adverb goes before the verb. 'I always forget.' Not 'I forget always.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I always forget. Okay. There are so many rules!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Yes, but you're learning them. Next question: what is your uncle's job?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My uncle... his job is... he is a... how to say 'abogado'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Abogado is 'lawyer'! 'He is a lawyer.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He is a lawyer! His office is in Valencia center... in the center of Valencia.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good correction! 'In the center of Valencia' - perfect word order. Okay, one more question for this game. Does your cousin play any sport?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My cousin Maria, she play... she plays football. Her team is very good!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Awesome! You self-corrected again! 'She plays football and her team is very good.' I love it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In Spain, everybody plays football. Is the national sport!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: IT is the national sport. And yes, I know! Spain is incredible at football. Now, speaking of Spain - do you miss the food?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh my God, yes! I miss the food so much! The food in England is... mmm... it is not very good. Sorry!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! No need to apologize, many people say that. What food do you miss the most?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I miss the paella of my grandmother. Her paella is the best in the world! And also I miss... es que... the tortilla espanola. In England they don't know tortilla.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: I miss your grandmother's paella too now! And you're right, tortilla espanola is hard to find in London. What about English food - have you tried fish and chips?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! Fish and chips is good. I like. But is very... how to say... graso?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Graso is 'greasy' or 'fatty'. 'It is very greasy.' And remember to say 'I like IT' - the object. In English we can't just say 'I like' alone.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: It is very greasy. I like it but I cannot eat every day. My stomach!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Fair enough. Okay, let's get back on track. We were talking about possessives. Let me teach you 'our' and 'their'. 'Our' is nuestro - for we. 'Our family is big.' And 'their' is su - for they. 'Their house is in Madrid.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Our family is big. Their house is in Madrid. Okay, I understand.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect. Now let's use them together. Tell me about your grandparents using 'their'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My grandparents live in a village. Their house is old but very beautiful. Their dog is... they don't have a dog. They have chickens!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! They have chickens - that's so great. 'Their house is old but beautiful' - beautiful sentence. And 'their chickens' - perfect possessive. Do you visit them often?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes, in Spain I visit... I visited them every weekend. But now I am in London, so I don't visit them. I call them in the phone.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great! Just one thing - we say 'ON the phone', not 'in the phone'. 'I call them on the phone.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: On the phone. Why 'on'? Is strange.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It is strange! Prepositions don't always translate logically. You just have to memorize them. 'On the phone', 'on the internet', 'on TV'. Okay, let's move to our next section. We're going to learn how to describe family members. Adjectives!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Adjectives! I like adjectives. I know some: big, small, good, bad...

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great start! For describing people, let's learn these important ones. Listen and repeat: tall, short, young, old, funny, nice, strict, kind, smart.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Tall, short, young, old, funny, nice, strict... how to say strict?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Strict means estricto. Like a teacher who gives a lot of homework and is very serious.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Ah, strict! Like my mathematics teacher in Spain. She was very strict.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! And then 'kind' means amable, and 'smart' means inteligente. Now, here's something really important about English adjectives. In English, the adjective goes BEFORE the noun. Not after, like in Spanish.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Before? So not 'the woman tall'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! Not 'the woman tall' - we say 'the tall woman'. In Spanish you say 'la mujer alta', but in English the adjective comes first.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: The tall woman. Okay. So... the old house? The big dog?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes! Perfect! 'The old house', 'the big dog'. Now let's practice with your family. Describe your sister Lucia.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My sister Lucia is... she is... my sister tall... no, wait. My tall sister. She is tall and very funny.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great self-correction! 'My tall sister' - adjective before noun. And 'she is tall and very funny' - beautiful. Tell me more about her.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She is tall and she has... the hair... long hair? Long brown hair. And she is very sympathetic with everyone.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ooh, careful! 'Sympathetic' is another false friend, like 'actually'. In English, 'sympathetic' means you feel sorry for someone's problems. If you want to say simpatica, the word is 'nice' or 'friendly'. 'She is very friendly.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Another false friend! She is very friendly. Not sympathetic. Oh, English is full of traps!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It really is! But now you know two important ones: 'actually' doesn't mean 'actualmente', and 'sympathetic' doesn't mean 'simpatico'. You'll remember because you made the mistake. Mistakes are the best teachers!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Mistakes are the best teachers. I like this. Okay, I continue with my family?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes please! Tell me about your brother Pablo.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My brother Pablo, he is twelve years old. He is short... no, he is not short, he is... medium? And he is very active. He play... he plays football every day.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Excellent! 'Medium height' is what we say. And good catch with 'plays'! Is Pablo nice or is he annoying? 'Annoying' means 'molesto'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh! Annoying! Yes, sometimes he is very annoying! He enter in my room and take my things.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Classic little brother! Remember: 'He enterS my room and takeS my things.' And we just say 'enters my room' - no 'in' needed.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He enters my room and takes my things. Yes! Brothers!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Now, describe your mother for me. What does she look like? What is her personality?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My mother is a woman very nice. She is not very tall, she is... media... medium height. She has dark hair and brown eyes.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good description! But remember the adjective order - 'a very nice woman', not 'a woman very nice'. The adjective comes before the noun in English.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: A very nice woman. My mother is a very nice woman. Is difficult to remember!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: IT is difficult, but you'll get it. It's the opposite of Spanish, so your brain wants to put the adjective after. Totally natural. Now what about your dad?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My father is tall and he is... how to say 'delgado'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Delgado is 'thin' or 'slim'. 'He is tall and slim.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He is tall and slim. He has short hair, a grey... gray? Hair. And he is very kind. He always help everyone.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: He always helpS everyone - that -s again! And 'grey' and 'gray' are both correct - 'grey' is British, 'gray' is American. Since you're in London, 'grey' works great!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He always helps everyone. I use the British English because I am in London!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Smart choice! Now describe your grandmother. You said she's funny?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! My grandma is very short and a little... gordita... how to say?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: In English we might say 'a little chubby' or 'plump'. These are gentle ways to say it.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She is a little chubby. And she is very funny. She always tells stories about when she was young. And she cook... she cooks the best food!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: She cooks the best food - good correction! Your grandmother sounds wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She is wonderful! I call her every Sunday on the phone. She don't use... she doesn't use video call because she is old and the technology is difficult for her.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great self-correction from 'don't' to 'doesn't'! You're getting better and better at catching those. Now let me ask you to describe someone using two adjectives. For example: 'My mother is kind and smart.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My mother is kind and smart. My father is kind and... patient? Paciente?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes! 'Patient' - same word almost! 'My father is kind and patient.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: And my sister is tall and funny. And my brother is short and annoying!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Poor Pablo. Okay, can you try three adjectives? 'My grandmother is short, chubby, and funny.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My grandmother is short, chubby, and funny. And also she is very old. She have... she has eighty years... no! She IS eighty years old!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Triple correction! You caught the 'have/has', AND you caught the 'have years' to 'is years old'! Carmen, you're on fire!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you! I am learning! My brain is... working hard today.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It definitely is! Okay, let me now ask you some comparing questions. Who is taller - your mother or your father?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My father is more tall than my mother.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good try! With short adjectives like 'tall', we don't say 'more tall'. We add -er: 'taller'. 'My father is taller than my mother.' But don't worry about comparatives too much today - we'll study those in another lesson.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My father is taller than my mother. Taller. Okay!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Now, who is the funniest person in your family?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: The funniest? My grandma! She is the funniest person in my family. She always makes us laugh.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Beautiful sentence! 'She always makes us laugh' - that's a really natural-sounding phrase. Great job.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: And my grandfather is the most quiet person. He like to read the newspaper and sit in his chair.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: The quietest - for short adjectives. But great description! 'He likes to read the newspaper and sit in his chair.' Remember 'likes' with the -s.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He likes to read. Yes. He is very tranquilo... quiet. He live in his own world.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: He LIVES in his own world. The -s! And 'he lives in his own world' is a great expression. You're starting to use English idioms!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He lives in his own world. Sorry, the -s, the -s! I know but I forget.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Don't apologize! It's the most common error at your level and it's completely normal. Even advanced Spanish speakers sometimes forget it. Keep going! Tell me, who is the strictest person in your family?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: The strictest? Hmm... I think my mother. She is very strict with the homework and the school. She always says 'Carmen, you need to study more!'

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! She sounds like a typical teacher-mom. Okay, brilliant work on describing family members. Let's now change topics. Let's talk about your home! Where you live.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh, I can talk about my home in Spain or my home in London?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Let's start with Spain and then talk about London! So your family home in Valencia - can you describe it?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! My house in Valencia is... a big house. Is not very big, but is nice. It has... es que... it has three bedrooms.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good! IT is not very big, but IT is nice - remember the 'it'. And great vocabulary - 'bedrooms'. Let me teach you some room words. Repeat after me: kitchen, living room, bedroom, bathroom, dining room.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Kitchen, living room, bedroom, bathroom, dining room. Kitchen is like 'cocina'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! Kitchen is cocina. Now, to describe what's in a room, we use 'there is' for one thing and 'there are' for many things. 'There is a bed in my bedroom.' 'There are two chairs in the kitchen.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: There is a bed in my bedroom. There are two chairs in the kitchen. This is like 'hay' in Spanish!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly like 'hay'! But in English we have two versions: 'there is' for singular and 'there are' for plural. Spanish just uses 'hay' for everything. Now, describe your kitchen.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My kitchen is big because my mother cooks a lot. There is a big table and there is many chairs.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Almost! 'There ARE many chairs' - many is plural, so we need 'are'. 'There are many chairs.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: There are many chairs. Because my family is big and we always eat together.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: That's lovely! Eating together is important in Spanish culture, right?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! Very important. In Spain, the lunch is at two o'clock, and the dinner is at nine or ten o'clock. In England is very different! Here the dinner is at six o'clock. Is too early!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: IT is very different and IT is too early - the 'it's again! But yes, the meal times are so different between Spain and England. Okay, let's continue with the rooms. Tell me about your bedroom in Spain.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: My bedroom in Spain is my favorite room. There is a big bed, and there is a desk where I study. There are many books. And I have a window big... a big window that has a view of the street.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Beautiful! You caught the adjective order with 'big window' - you almost said 'window big' but fixed it. And 'there is a desk where I study' - very nice structure.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you! And my sister has the bedroom next to my bedroom. Her bedroom is very... disorganizado... messy?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes! 'Messy' is the word. 'Her bedroom is very messy.' Great vocabulary!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Her bedroom is very messy! She has clothes in the floor, in the bed, in everywhere!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Almost - 'clothes ON the floor, ON the bed, everywhere' - no 'in' needed before 'everywhere'. And for surfaces like floors and beds, we use 'on', not 'in'. 'On the floor, on the bed.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: On the floor, on the bed. 'On' for surfaces. Okay, I try to remember.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good. Now let me teach you some furniture words. In a bedroom you have: a bed, a desk, a chair, a wardrobe - do you know 'wardrobe'?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Wardrobe... like armario? Where you put the clothes?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! Wardrobe is armario. And in a living room you might have: a sofa, a TV, a bookshelf, a coffee table. Can you repeat those?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Sofa, TV, bookshelf, coffee table. A coffee table is a table small... a small table?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes! A small table, usually in front of the sofa. And you caught the adjective order again - 'a small table'. You're getting better at that.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: A small table in front of the sofa. In my living room in Spain there is a big sofa and a television very big... a very big television.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good correction! 'A very big television.' And you used 'there is' correctly. What about the bathroom?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In the bathroom there is... a shower, a toilet, a... how to say 'lavabo'?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Lavabo is 'sink' in English. Or 'basin'. 'There is a shower, a toilet, and a sink.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: A sink! And there is a mirror. I like looking in the mirror.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Great vocabulary! 'Mirror' - espejo. Now, let's talk about your home in London. You live in a flat, right? An apartment?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! I live in a flat with other students. We are four people in the flat.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Four people! Wow. Is it a big flat?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: No! It is very small! My room is very small. There is only a bed and a desk. There is not space for more things.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: There ISN'T space, or 'there is NO space'. Both work! Tell me about the kitchen in your flat.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: The kitchen is shared. We share the kitchen. It is always dirty because my flatmates don't clean. Es que... it is very frustrating!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Welcome to shared student living! Good use of 'frustrating'. And tell me about the living room.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: There is a small living room with a sofa old... an old sofa. And a television. We watch TV together sometimes.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: An old sofa - great correction! 'There is a small living room with an old sofa.' Now how about we compare the two homes? Spain versus London.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh, they are very different! My house in Spain is big and beautiful. The house big... the big house has a garden and is beautiful. My flat in London is small and... feo... ugly?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes, 'ugly' works, or maybe 'not very nice'. And you caught the adjective order halfway - you said 'the house big' and then 'the big house'. Remember: adjective first!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: The big house. My big house in Spain has a garden. But my small flat in London doesn't have a garden.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Beautiful comparison! 'My big house in Spain has a garden but my small flat in London doesn't have a garden.' That's really good sentence structure.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you! And also, in Spain I have my own bathroom. But in London I share the bathroom with three persons... three people.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Good catch with 'people'! 'I share the bathroom with three people.' That's tough! How many bathrooms are in the flat?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: There is only one bathroom for four people. There are many problems in the morning because everyone need to use it.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Everyone NEEDS - with 'everyone' we use the same form as he/she. 'Everyone needs to use it.' But very well described!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Everyone needs to use it. And there is always hair in the... in the shower. Is disgusting!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: IT is disgusting! And yes, shared bathrooms are no fun. Now let me ask you - if you could change one thing about your London flat, what would it be?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I want a room more big... a bigger room! And my own bathroom.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: A bigger room - yes! Good. Okay, let me teach you one more useful structure for describing rooms. 'There is a...' and 'there are some...' For example: 'In my room there is a bed and there are some posters on the wall.' Can you make a sentence like that?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In my room in London there is a small bed and there are some photos of my family on the wall.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect sentence! Photos of your family on the wall - that must be nice to have.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes, I look at them when I miss my family. And I have a photo of Chico, my dog!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Aw, Chico! Now let me give you some more useful phrases for describing a house. 'Upstairs' and 'downstairs'. Do you know these?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Upstairs is... arriba? And downstairs is abajo?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! 'The bedrooms are upstairs and the kitchen is downstairs.' Can you describe your house in Spain using upstairs and downstairs?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In my house in Spain, the kitchen and the living room are downstairs. And the bedrooms and the bathroom are upstairs. And there is a... terrace? In the... arriba... upstairs.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: A terrace upstairs - that sounds amazing. Or we could say 'a rooftop terrace'. Very common in Spain, not so much in London!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! A rooftop terrace. We eat there in summer. It is very nice with the sunset.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: That sounds wonderful. I'm jealous! Okay, let's do a quick activity. I'm going to ask you 'What's in your...' and you tell me. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Ready!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: What's in your kitchen in Spain?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In my kitchen there is a big table, there are six chairs, there is a refrigerator, and there is a window with a view of the garden.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Excellent! Very detailed. What's in your living room?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In my living room there is a big sofa, a small coffee table, a very big television, and there are many books on the bookshelf. And there are photos of the family on the wall.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Beautiful description! You're using 'there is' and 'there are' perfectly. What about your parents' bedroom?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I don't go in my parents' bedroom much! But there is a big bed, a wardrobe, and there is a small bathroom inside. How to say... a bathroom private?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: A private bathroom - adjective before the noun! Or we say 'en suite' bathroom. It's a French word that English uses. 'There is an en suite bathroom.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: A private bathroom. An en suite. A car red... no! A red car. I am practicing!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! You caught yourself! Yes, 'a red car', not 'a car red'. Your brain is learning! Okay, this is great. Let me also teach you one more room - do you know 'garage'?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Garage! Like garaje in Spanish. We have a garage. My father park... parks his car there.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: My father parks his car there - perfect with the -s! Good. And 'garden' - you already know that one. 'Jardin'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! We have a beautiful garden with flowers and a lemon tree!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: A lemon tree! That's so Spanish. You don't see many lemon trees in London, do you?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: No! Is too cold for lemon trees in London. But I see many... how to say... robles? Big trees?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Roble is 'oak tree'. 'There are many oak trees in London.' And IT is too cold - remember our 'it'. Okay Carmen, we've covered so much today! Let's do a review now.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Already? The time passes very fast!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It does! That's because you're having fun. Time flies when you're having fun - that's an English expression. Okay, review time! I'm going to do a little quiz. First, family vocabulary. Tell me five family members.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Mother, father, sister, grandmother, uncle!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Five more!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Brother, grandfather, aunt, cousin, nephew!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: All correct! Now, possessive adjectives. Fill in the blank: 'My father, ___ name is Carlos.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: His! His name is Carlos.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: 'My mother, ___ job is teacher.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Her! Her job is teacher. Her job is to be a teacher?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: We'd say 'Her job is teaching' or 'She is a teacher.' Both work! Now rooms. Tell me three rooms in a house.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Kitchen, bedroom, bathroom!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Three more!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Living room, dining room, and... garage!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Now, furniture. What furniture is in a bedroom?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In a bedroom there is a bed, a desk, a chair, and a wardrobe.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: All correct! And what furniture is in a living room?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: In a living room there is a sofa, a coffee table, a television, and a bookshelf.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect! Okay, now let's do the tricky part. I'm going to say something with a mistake, and you need to correct it. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh, I am the teacher now? Okay, ready!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Yes, you're the teacher now. Correct this: 'She have two brothers.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She HAS two brothers!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Correct! Now this one: 'Is very cold today.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: IT is very cold today! The 'it'!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Yes! Now correct this: 'I have a car red.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I have a red car! Adjective before the noun!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Excellent! And this one: 'There is many books.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: There ARE many books. Because 'many' is plural.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: You're getting all of them! One more: 'He live in Madrid.'

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: He LIVES in Madrid. The -s!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Carmen, you got every single one correct! I'm really impressed with your progress today.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you Miguel! I feel that I learn a lot today.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: You did! Now let me give you a little summary of what we covered. Today we learned: family vocabulary - mother, father, sister, brother, grandmother, grandfather, aunt, uncle, cousin, nephew, niece, husband, wife. We learned possessive adjectives - my, your, his, her, our, their. We practiced adjectives for describing people - tall, short, young, old, funny, nice, strict, kind, smart. And we learned room vocabulary and 'there is' and 'there are'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: That is a lot of things!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It is! And you also learned some important grammar rules. Can you tell me the rules we practiced?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Hmm... the 'it' in the beginning of the sentence. Like 'it is cold'. And the -s for he and she. And the adjective before the noun.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! Those are the three big ones. And also 'there is' for singular and 'there are' for plural. And age - 'she IS twenty years old', not 'she HAS twenty years'. These are all things that are different from Spanish.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes, English and Spanish are very different sometimes. But I like English! It is a beautiful language.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It is! And you're making amazing progress. Now, for homework this week, I want you to do three things.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Homework! Okay, I am ready.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Number one: write a short paragraph describing your family. Use at least five family words and five adjectives. And use possessives - his, her, their.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Okay, a paragraph about my family with adjectives and possessives. I can do this.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Number two: draw a map of your flat in London and label all the rooms in English. Write one sentence about each room using 'there is' or 'there are'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: A map of my flat! That is fun. There is a kitchen, there is a bathroom...

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly like that! And number three: learn the false friends we talked about. 'Actually' does not mean 'actualmente' - it means 'en realidad'. 'Sympathetic' does not mean 'simpatico' - it means feeling sorry. Write three sentences using these words correctly.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Okay, three homeworks. I will do them this week. Actually... I will do them today! I used 'actually' correctly?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Yes, but only if you mean 'en realidad, voy a hacerlos hoy.' If you mean 'currently', then no! Which did you mean?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I mean... hmm... I mean 'en realidad'! I really want to do them today because I am excited about learning.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Then yes, 'actually' is correct! Well done. And one more thing - please practice the third person -s. It's the thing Spanish speakers forget the most.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: She works, he lives, it rains. I will practice!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Perfect examples! Okay Carmen, do you have any questions before we finish?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes, I have one question. How old she is? No, wait... How old is she? My flatmate Anna. She say... she says she is twenty but I think she is more old.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Good self-correction on the question order. 'How old IS she' - the verb comes before the subject in questions. And we say 'older', not 'more old'. 'I think she is older.' But maybe don't tell her that!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I think she is older. Ha! No, I don't tell her. She is very nice and I don't want to be... mmm... grosera?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Grosera is 'rude' in English. 'I don't want to be rude.' Very good!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I don't want to be rude. Thank you Miguel! This class was very good. I learn... I learned a lot today.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: I'm glad! You were amazing today. Your energy is fantastic and you're not afraid to make mistakes, which is the best way to learn.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Thank you! I am agree with... no! I agree with you. In Spanish we say 'estoy de acuerdo' but in English is just 'I agree', yes?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Exactly! Another great catch! In Spanish it's 'estoy de acuerdo' - with the verb estar, so Spanish speakers often say 'I am agree.' But in English it's just 'I agree.' No 'am'.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I agree. Just 'I agree'. English is more simple sometimes!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: It can be! And sometimes it's more complicated. That's languages for you. Okay Carmen, same time next week?

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Yes! Same time next week. Next week we can talk about more things? I want to learn about my university and my classes.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Absolutely! Next week we'll talk about daily routines and your university life. It will be great.

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh, I love this! I am very excited! Ay, 'excited' is correct in English, yes? It is not a false friend?

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Ha! Great question. 'Excited' IS correct in English - it means 'emocionada'. It's NOT the same as 'excitada' in Spanish, which has a different meaning. So yes, 'I am excited' is perfect and safe to use!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Oh good! Because I say 'I am excited' very much. Okay, thank you Miguel! Have a good week!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: You too Carmen! Don't forget your homework. And keep watching Netflix in English - it's really helping!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: I watch Netflix every day! Currently I watch a series about... a family! So I practice the vocabulary.

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: Look at you using 'currently' correctly! Amazing. Okay, bye Carmen! See you next week!

utterances.speaker: Carmen
utterances.text: Bye Miguel! See you next week! Have a nice day!

utterances.speaker: Miguel
utterances.text: You too! Bye!
